card.title: How Composting Actually Works
card.teaser: Your kitchen scraps don't just rot — billions of microbes are running a controlled fire without flames.
card.coverImagePrompt: A cross-section of a steaming compost pile at golden hour, coffee grounds, eggshells, and autumn leaves layered in rich brown earth, faint wisps of steam rising, macro detail, warm cinematic lighting

lessons.title: A pile that gets hot by itself
lessons.body: Stick a thermometer into a working compost pile and you might read 140°F (60°C) — hotter than a cup of coffee. No one lit a match. The heat comes from trillions of microbes eating your banana peels and lawn clippings so fast that their metabolism cooks the pile from the inside.

visual.imagePrompt: A gardener's hand holding a compost thermometer reading 140°F, steam rising from a dark pile of vegetable scraps

lessons.title: Greens and browns
lessons.body: Every successful compost pile balances two ingredients: 'greens' (nitrogen-rich stuff like vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, grass clippings) and 'browns' (carbon-rich stuff like dry leaves, cardboard, straw). Microbes need both — nitrogen to build proteins, carbon for energy. The sweet spot is roughly 25 to 30 parts carbon for every 1 part nitrogen.

keyTerms.definition: Wet, nitrogen-rich materials like food scraps and grass
Dry, carbon-rich materials like leaves, straw, and cardboard
The ratio of carbon to nitrogen that drives microbial activity

visual.imagePrompt: Split-screen: vibrant green vegetable scraps on one side, dry brown leaves and cardboard on the other, balanced on a rustic scale

lessons.title: Two shifts of microbes
lessons.body: Composting happens in stages. First, mesophilic bacteria (active at 50–100°F) move in and start breaking down sugars and proteins. As they multiply, their heat drives the temperature up — and hands off the pile to thermophilic bacteria, which thrive at 100–160°F and demolish tougher materials like cellulose. Finally, as fuel runs out, the pile cools and fungi and worms finish the job.

keyTerms.definition: Microbes that thrive at moderate temperatures

visual.imagePrompt: Microscopic illustration of bacteria swarming food scraps, with a temperature gradient graph showing the hot composting phase

lessons.title: The turn-it-or-lose-it rule
lessons.body: Microbes need oxygen. When a pile goes untouched, the center runs out of air and anaerobic bacteria take over — producing methane and that rotten-egg smell. USDA studies show that turning a pile every 3–7 days can cut composting time from a year down to about 6 weeks while keeping it odor-free.
lessons.funFact: A properly managed hot pile can kill most weed seeds and pathogens — which is why farmers compost manure before spreading it on crops.

visual.imagePrompt: A gardener mid-motion turning a steaming compost pile with a pitchfork, dark earthy mixture spilling over, late afternoon light

lessons.title: Rot is alive
lessons.body: What looks like 'rotting garbage' is actually one of the most complex ecosystems you'll ever build. A spoonful of finished compost contains more microbes than there are humans on Earth. Composting isn't waste disposal — it's ranching invisible livestock that turn your trash into the best soil amendment money can't buy.

quiz.question: What roughly is the ideal carbon-to-nitrogen ratio for a compost pile?
quiz.explanation: Microbes work best when there's about 25–30 parts carbon (browns) for every 1 part nitrogen (greens).

quiz.question: Why does a healthy compost pile get hot?
options: Chemical reactions in the food scraps themselves
quiz.explanation: Thermophilic bacteria eat so voraciously that their collective metabolism heats the pile to 140°F or more.

quiz.question: Why do gardeners turn their compost?
options: To bring oxygen to the microbes
quiz.explanation: Turning introduces oxygen so aerobic microbes can keep working — preventing smelly anaerobic decomposition.

sources.title: On-Farm Composting Handbook (NRAES-54)
sources.publisher: USDA / Natural Resource Agriculture and Engineering Service
